text:p.text:span: This letter presents unique challenges for the editor. In the past, two different redactions have been considered separate letters but upon reflection I have decided to edit them together. The main reason for separating them was their sequence one after the other on a single tablet (X1/X3) but this should not preclude us from considering them as different versions of the same composition.

The redactional history of the letter is undoubtedly complex, and we only have glimpses of the long history of the permutations of the text. The four earlier sources of unknown provenience can be grouped into two sets that have been edited separately here. The letter that it answers, PuÅ1 (letter 13), is known from four Nippur witnesses and one from Kish, in addition to four of undetermined origin, and it is more than probable that ÅPu1 was concocted outside of Nippur to create a pair with that epistle. One of these is X4, which may be MB; see the discussion of this tablet on p.
Su1S/A) with syllabic Sumerian dates to the fourteenth or fifteenth century (see p.

above), and seems to be derived from the tradition of OB1a, with the additions of lines 9–19; the first part of this section, lines 9–14, were lifted literally from ArÅ2 (part B, letter 3). The differences between the two OB traditions appear to be concentrated at the beginning and end of the letters. Moreover, interpretation is often made difficult by the idiosyncratic Sumerian orthography of
. The scribe of this tablet uses unusual values, often indicates crasis but also plays with different ways of writing the same word. The Akkadian version shows definite signs of post-OB composition.

office:text.text:p: Tablet typology: compilation tablet: X1, X2 Type III (landscape).

text:note-body.text:p: The corresponding line numbers in the Middle Babylonian version (ŠPŠMB1) are indicated in the right hand column.

text:p.text:span: 7. X1 Ñá-e x[…] x x x x […]
This is the last line of col. iii; there are at least x lines broken at the beginning of col. iv.
Speak to Puzur-Åulgi, general of Bad-IgihursaÑa,
saying (the words of) Åulgi, your king:
An­ and Enlil gave me to rule all the lands and far-flung ­peoples.
the cities of the homeland, province by province,
as well as the peoples of the far-flung homeland rested in safe ­pastures.
Have­ the governor(s) and the prefect(s) [
wherever the fortification has deteriorated, it must be restored,
(the repairs to) the fallen sides (of the wall)
must be finished in the course of one month!
] for a report) about this work.
And now the the Tidnumites have returned to (help) me from the highlands.
He will have his own [travel provision]s for you, ­
then, after Lu-Nanna, the governor of Zimudar
has left both of you with his worker troops,
you and Aradmu must not cease supervising/
and you must not neglect the
The work must progress day and night;
neither of you is to sleep by night or day—you must both know about this!
The synchronization of the two OB sources is provisional at best, the reconstruction of lines 21–24 is particularly suspect, because the texts may have differed at this point.
. This line makes little sense here and is not present in any other version; it seems to have been adopted from line 33 of a non-Nippur version of IbPu1 (24):
The difference is in the verbal root, which seems to be
in the present text. This is probably due to the fact that Akkadian
, the plural perfect root of the verb
, “to go.” All the other versions of this line have

office:text.text:p: Tablet typology: both compilation tablets (Type I).

text:note-body.text:p: The corresponding line numbers in the Middle Babylonian version (ŠPŠMB1) are indicated in the right hand column.

text:p.text:span: 6. X3[ùÑ kalam daÑal-la ú sa]l-la nú-dè7
Speak to Puzur-Åulgi, general of Bad-IgihursaÑa,
saying (the words of) Åulgi, your king:
When the great fortifications of Bad-IgihursaÑa were being­ built—
so that the Amorites could not descend on the homeland,­
nor water (their herds) at the banks of the Abgal as well as the Tigris and Euphrates,
that [the people of the­ far-flung homeland] rest in sa[fe pastures],
and not be terrori[zed in their dwellings],
let them mobilize [all their villages, one by one]
and appoint [the man in charge of the construction].
[The fortifications must be torn down], and then rebuilt.
After I consult you about this undertaking,
Lu-Nanna, governor of Zimudar province is to come to you with his worker troops.
You (sing.) [should not allow the worker troops] to leave.
These lines are clearly a paraphrase of ÅaÅu1: 4–7 (118)
igi-zu ma-an-Ñar-ma amurru ma-da-aå mu-un-åub-åub-bu-uå, bàd dù-ù-dè Ñìri-bi ku
buranun-na-bi-da, gú-Ñìri-bi a-åà-e nam-ba-e-åú-åú á-åè mu-e-da-áÑ
… and presented your views to me as follows: “The Amorites have repeatedly raided the province.”­ You commanded me

to rebuild the fortifications, to cut off their­ (infiltration) route, thus to prevent them from swooping down on the fields through­ a breach (in the defenses) between the Tigris and Euphrates.” Note that these lines are absent in all other versions; moreover, they clearly contradict ÅPu1OB1a: 19 above.
Su1S = Susa A XX/1 1962/3, Sumerian version (
Note that the photograph of the edge in
Su1A = Susa A XX/1 1962/3, Akkadian version (ibid.) 4–41

office:text.text:p: The Susa tablet is in Tehran and was collated from photographs kindly provided by Shahrokh Razmjou and Mahboubeh Ghelichkhani with the help of Parsa Daneshmand. X2 is possibly from Sippar.

text:p.text:span: : Edition of ÅPu1MB1, D. O. Edzard,

In the comparative matrix, * marks ­an ideal reconstructed Middle Babylonian version. The­ reconstruction is intended only as an aid in understanding the interpretation and translation of the syllabic text, and not as a­ posited original version.
The first two signs look more like
un kalam daÑal-la ú sal-la ì-nú
un kalam dam-gal-lá ú sal-lá i-nu
mi dru åû quttû øuppakunu lillika
Speak to Puzur-Åulgi, general of Bad-IgihursaÑa,
saying (the words of) Åulgi, your king:
An­ and Enlil gave me to rule all the foreign lands and the far-flung­ multitudes;
all the various cities, province by province,
as well as the peoples of the far-flung homeland rested in safe ­pastures.
I made them dwell in far-flung places and peaceful­ habitations.
As for their men and women,
their man goes wherever he wishes,
spindles and­ needles, wandering on whatever roads they wish.
Having established their animal pens on the far-flung desert,
and pitched their tents and encampments all over­,
the laborers and tenant farmers then spend their days (working) in the­ fields.
Proudly I ordered the wall-work on the fortification!
For the well-being of my army and of my frontier territory,
I made ­inquiries, I consulted about bringing about peace, and (decided to) (re)build this fortification.
and­ so make the wall builders do that work!
And now I am (also) sending Aradmu to you.
Have­ the farmers and the overseers bring their people,
mobilize all their cities for you,
the man in charge of the­ work.
Wherever the wall has deteriorated, it must be ­restored,
the fortifications must be finished in one month!
You/I will then ask (Aradmu for a report) about this work.
is coming to you with his worker troops.
He will have his own [travel provision]s for you, ­
and he should not complain to you.
When the fortifications are finished, then and you shall both send your written report (to me).
When both of you have inspected (the work), ­
and after Lu-Nanna, governor of Zimudar
has left you ­both with his worker troops,
The work must progress day and night,
and you must not neglect the important orders
To Puzur-Åulgi, the general of Bad-IgihursaÑa,­ speak:
An­ and Enlil gave me to rule all the foreign lands and the far-flung­ multitudes;
then all the individual cities, province by ­province,
as well as the peoples of the far-flung lands I made ­rest in safe pastures,
I did not allow anyone to harass them in their homes.
As for their men and women,
their (lit. its) man goes ­wherever he wishes,
and the woman with her spindle and­ <her> needles <>.
when she filled the far-flung desert with
The ­laborers and cultivators spend their days (working) in the fields.
The farmer(s) and the overseer(s) are to do all the work that is required on that fortification.
Proudly I ordered the work on the fortification!
For my own well-being and well-being of my land/frontier territory,
I made inquiries; I consulted about bringing about peace, and (decided to) (re)build the fortification.
and so make the wall-builders perform the task of (re)building the ­fortification!
And now (also) I have sought out Aradmu (for you).
The farmers(s) and the overseer(s) have­ mobilized the villages for you on my behalf, one by one, as much as the (work on) this fortification requires.
May the man in charge of the work <> to the house.
Wherever the fortification has deteriorated, it must be restored,
that fortification must be­ finished in one month!
Make the workers perform the task of building the ­fortification!
I have dispatched to you­ Lu-Nanna, governor of Zimudar province, together with his worker-troops. ­
He is bringing you his own travel provisions, ­
and he should not complain to you.
When the fortification is finished, then and you shall both send your written report to me.
After you have both personally inspected (the work), ­
then leave behind Lu-Nanna, governor of Zimudar, together with­ his worker troops,
but you and Aradmu should not remain behind!
The work must progress day and night!
Your assignment is of vital importance, your ….
is the case with all known bilingual letters, except for the

, in MB1 the Akkadian does not render the opening formula. Here, however, unlike in the other letter on the same tablet (ÅIå1, 15), the first line of the body of the message is also not rendered into Akkadian.
is definitely post-OB; see Aa II/4 153–54 (
Ñidri níÑ-si-sá åu-ni-åè Ñá kur-kur daga[l nam-en-na-aå mu-na-an]-åúm-uå
. The scribe of Su1S was inconsistent in recognizing Sumerian
in this line. As an example of the different realizations of the same word, compare this with

office:text.text:p: 8. The Sumerian and Akkadian do not agree here. As D. O. Edzard,­ MDAI 57 25, has noted, the Akkadian version of lines 8–9 is similar to CH XXV­: niåī dadmī aburrī uåarbiœ mugallitam ul uåaråiåinãti. Indeed, this may be the very source of this line. Interestingly, the other version of the letter seems to have had a Sumerian line corresponding more closely to the Akkadian of this version (ÅPu1 OB1: 6–7) [ùÑ kalam daÑal-la ú s]al-la nú-dè [ki-tuå nu-h]u?-luh-e-dè. The hu sign may actually be dù, which would connect dù-luh, “troublemaker,” with our passage. The word is rare; it is attested late with the equivalent mu-gal- [li-tu] in Antagal B 209 [MSL 17 193] but also in an unusual OB lexical text from Uœar-Lulu (Tell Dhibāºī): lú dù-luh-ha (AOAT 25, 3 vi 9). It must be from hu-luh (galtu), and is so documented in a similar Sumerian passage from a Hammurabi hymn (TLB 2 3: 23 [ZA 54 (1962) 52]): ≠ma-da-mu ú-sal-la mi-ni-in-nú un-gá lú-hu-luh-ha nu-mu-ni-in-tuku, and even more appropriately, in a royal inscription of Samsuiluna (E4.3.7.8: 61–62, D. Frayne, RIME 4 390 but see A. George, BSOAS 55 [1992] 539): ki-tuå ne-ha tuå-ù-dè lú hu-luh-ha nu-tuk-tuku-dè = åu-ba-at ne-[eæ-tim] a-na åú-åu-b[i-im] m[u-gal-li-tam a-na la åu-ur-åe-em].

text:p.text:span: is characteristic of Isin, Larsa and Babylon I royal texts and occurs also in the
. This passage is not in the OB version, and may have been introduced in stages into the composition. Lines 9–14 were borrowed from

letter ArÅ2: B3’–7’ (letter 3; for comparison, the ArÅ2 composite text is provided in the matrix). Then, the following passage is bracketed by two almost identical lines (15=22), both possibly corresponding to line 15 of ÅPuOB1a. This is a trace of a rather crude editorial process.

The scribe clearly had problems with the source text; he abbreviated the Sumerian, which he clearly did not understand, and therefore did not render the end of the line into Akkadian. One should note that the writing

cannot be used as evidence for the reconstruction of the final consonant of the Sumerian word for “woman,” as the Susa text frequently uses various /å/ signs for /s/, and the playful orthography cannot be used as evidence for any language norms.
. The context makes it easy to make a guess as to the meaning ­of
the other available evidence is­ ambiguous; see J. Krecher,
153. A clue to the meaning of the word­ may be found, perhaps, in
15 18: 37 and its unpublished duplicate CBS 145 rev. 6’ (cited by M. Civil,
, “a­ man captured a raven in his dwelling.” Here the syllabic text substitutes
. I am therefore assuming that the writing
. This is, then, another writing of
, “sleeping quarters.” Perhaps the distinction is one of human versus animal shelter. Here the Sumerian has the
, which may be an attempt to render
, “shelter, shed” a word that is, with two exceptions, otherwise only known from post-OB contexts; in lexical texts it translates
The only OB attestation is in two PN’s, see
T 27. I am grateful to Martha Roth for providing me with a copy of the entry prior to publication.
It is difficult to understand how the Sumerian verbal root
; perhaps the writer recognized some resonance of the completely unrelated expression
are not common in Sumerian. Here they are used in hendiadys
4 117]; this must be derived from Proto-Izi I 336–37 [

. As things now stand, neither of these lines is securely attested in the OB version; the restoration of ÅPuOB1a: 15 is most uncertain. With the exception of the first two words, I cannot interpret the Sumerian of line 15. The word
but the lines are clearly corrupt.

In both 15 and 22 the Akkadian line writes the two occupations as IM.SIG and ÅÀ.AB.RA.A, using two unique logographic writings, and the same signs are used in the Sumerian version as well. The scribe of this tablet writes three signs in a similar fashion: UD, ÉREN, and SIG but maintains distinctions. There can be little doubt that
, “overseer, administrator,” but the word has essentially gone out of use already in the later part of the OB period (N. Yoffee,
The Economic Role of the Crown
Å1 14), and it is probable that the MB writer did not know very much about this administrative title. It is possible that the source of this is the proximity of the words
. Edzard plausibly reconstructed the end of the Sumerian line as
and translated it as “Cette muraille ne doit pas diminuer son prestige.”
. These line seem to refer to PuÅ1:
zi ugnim ù kalam-ma-ni-åè bàd gal igi-hur-saÑ-Ñá mu lú kúr hul-Ñál-åè ùÑ kalam-ma-ni-åè mu-un-dù
, “Is it not for the sake of his own well-being that my king fashions (objects) of precious metals and jewels for the gods?
My king, for the well-being of the army and his country

has ­built the great fortifications of Igihursaga against the vile enemy for the sake of his people and his country.” This is the only allusion in this letter to the missive that it was designed to answer.
. The Sumerian of this line is difficult to understand.
in line 27, and the ­verb must be
, although possible there is contamination here with
, which may have been interpreted as
. More probably, at some point in the transmission process
was rendered by the Emesal form
. Note the rare use of the D-stem of
, which is already present in the OB version a, must be a back translation from Akkadian.
The Susa text appears to be garbled. While the Sumerian can be harmonized with the OB version, the Akkadian makes little sense. Instead of a verb that corresponds to
; in the orthography used here, this should correspond to
, which makes little sense in this context.
. The OB and the later text differ here, once again. The older text has
is not attested in any narrative but is found once in a lexical text:
renders as “limp arm” (M/1 255) but which must refer to the side of the wall here; note
two lines later in the same lexical text.
It is possible that this is an unusual back translation of
in Sumerian, and that the line should be rendered “the delayed work must be finished in the course of one month.”
. In this line X2 and Su1S agree on the Sumerian verb but the Akkadian has a completely differe
. These lines are clearly derived and adapted from the Åu-Sin correspondence; see the commentary to lines 26–29 of the previous letter (PÅu1, 13).
15 45], although the reading in the left hand column is broken, so there may have been a Diri-reading of the sign combination). The syllabic text of Su1S has
seems at odds with this. Edzard suggested that this might have been a composite­ beginning with
; more likely it is a pseudo-loan in Sumerian from
. Edzard reconstructed the underlying text as
(T 326) recognized that the first word was
, “complaint.” The Sumerian word is otherwise known only from lexical sources and from the post-OB
: 52. The OB version 1a 23, although broken at the
“to complain,” an idiom well attested in OB letters, which is found in the Akkadian version of the Susa text. The latter, however, has the verbal form

. The verbs in both lines differ from what is found in the OB versions; somewhere in the chain of transmission misunderstandings crept in that were then resolved by somewhat convoluted means. Already in the
in X2. Is one a hearing mistake for the other, or has there been a reinterpretation? In either case, it is difficult to decide which version, if any, might be primary. If
is to be taken literally, and not as a syllabic rendering, then it is probably used here similarly to Akkadian

M/1 433. It is also possible that it means “do not allow the (work) to slacken.” In view of the syllabic writing on the later version, it is unclear which of the two was passed down. Most probably it is
is possibly derived from a confusion with
, “to dig,” an explanation that is not out of line with other interpretations found in the text. The whole issue was complicated by the fact that the two Akkadian verbs,
, are both associated with the same Sumerian roots in the lexical tradition (mostly Diri):
[forthcoming, courtesy Martha Roth]. This is where the combination of
. There was clearly some transmission confusion about these lines, which are only in one of the two extant OB witnesses, albeit in reverse order from what we find in the MB version.
(see the dictionaries); note the post-Ur III letter
, “The watch must not come down from the wall night and day!” It is possible that idiomatically this might refer to periods when work is not normally done, to the night as well as daily siesta (
), as suggested to me by J. Cooper.
. The Sumerian and Akkadian versions of the Susa text are ­clearly corrupt. Somewhere along the line of transmission,
but the rest of it was misunderstood. Somehow
, “hand,” but then the interpretation came to a dead end, and the rest of the line seems to be incomplete. The scribe finished the line with
, then realized that this should be a separate line, inscribed the Akkadian translation, and then repeated the word, translating it defectively (<

office:text.text:p: Transliteration of the MB Susa Manuscript

text:p.text:span: 10. ùÑ kalam dam-gal-lá ú sal-lá i-nu
34. ne-et-te åi-li-ma U ÉÅ ZI-a ù
